title: Hormuz de-escalation vs. ship attack reality — May 2026
narrative: The US announced it was pausing strikes and entering indirect nuclear talks with Iran. Trump officials declared the Hormuz shipping lane "secure enough." Meanwhile, commercial tanker operators and seafarer unions report zero change in real danger on the water.
telemetry: Chinese supertanker carrying 2 million barrels of Iraqi crude transited the Strait of Hormuz on May 13 — after being stranded for days prior, requiring a convoy/escort to exit.
JV Innovation (Chinese-owned tanker) attacked near Hormuz — first confirmed Chinese tanker hit in the current conflict cycle.
Instagram footage from marine operators: 11 seafarers killed across reported attacks.
38 ship attacks reported in the current conflict cycle, per seafarer union and marine operator reports.
~20,000 crew still trapped or unable to transit the Strait, per maritime union briefings.
Reuters AIS data (May 13) shows supertanker 'exited' only after convoy/escort — not freely.
analysis: Official US statements of de-escalation are not matched by AIS telemetry or conditions on the water. A 'pause' in strikes is not the same as a safe passage corridor. The Chinese supertanker required military escort to transit — suggesting the threat assessment inside the government differs from its public posture.
verdict: [SIPHONED VERDICT] Official de-escalation unsupported by AIS data and seafarer union reports. Attacks continue post-pause. 'Secure enough' is not 'secure.'
